meta.originalTitle: The life of Beatrix Potter 彼得兔作家

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13, which are based on Reading Passage 1 below.
The children's author and illustrator who created Peter Rabbit

Beatrix Potter is one of the best-selling children's authors of all time. Her popular series of books that includes The Tale of Peter Rabbit was first published over one hundred years ago and has never been out of print. Generations of children have enjoyed her many stories and illustrations, all of which celebrate the English way of life and rural landscape through the adventures of animals.

Born into a comfortable middle-class family in London in 1866, Potter spent much of her early life in her own company. She was educated at home by a governess and rarely saw her brother, Bertram, who was sent to boarding school. Having little social contact with children of her own age, Potter was drawn into a private world of writing. When she was 15, she began to keep a diary written in a secret code of her own invention. Even Beatrix herself, when she read back over it in later life, found it difficult to understand. It was not until 15 years after her death that the code was cracked. To the outside world Beatrix seemed shy and reserved, but in her diary she expressed herself freely and was critical of the work of several contemporary artists.

Potter was a naturally gifted artist, and with the aid of some art lessons she also learned the technical side of drawing. Throughout her childhood she looked after many animals—rabbits, frogs and even bats—and sketched them constantly, gradually improving her work by sketching in museums. She was also interested in natural history. She would spend many hours drawing wildlife such as fungi and flowers, and at one time she had an ambition to develop this scientific interest. An uncle tried to help her enrol at the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew in London, but she was rejected because of her gender. Nevertheless, she won respect from the scientific establishment for her illustrations and her contribution to mycology, the study of fungi.

When Potter was in her early twenties, her parents tried to arrange a husband for her. Many suitable suitors were found; however, Potter turned them all down. Fiercely independent, she disliked the idea of being tied to a domestic life that, at that time, consisted mostly of staying at home and raising children. Thus—unusually for British women of the period—she remained single and lived in her parents' home.

For several years Potter tried to get her first children's book, The Tale of Peter Rabbit, published. Her initial attempts were unsuccessful, but she persevered, and eventually Frederick Warne & Co. accepted the book. It was finally published in 1902, when Potter was 36, though the publishers did not expect it to sell many copies. The project was given to the youngest brother in the firm, Norman Warne, as his first assignment—essentially a test. Luckily, he warmed to both the book and its author. Determined to make it a success, he worked closely with Potter, poring over every detail. It was Norman who insisted that each illustration should be in colour, while Potter insisted the book remain small enough for children to hold easily. By the end of the year, 28,000 copies were in print.

The relationship between Warne and Potter blossomed, and they eventually became engaged. However, Potter's parents disapproved because of his occupation. They relented only on condition that the couple live apart for six months to give Potter time to reconsider. Tragically, before the wedding could take place, Warne developed pernicious anaemia, a blood disease, and passed away. Potter was devastated and wrote to his sister, Millie, “He did not live long, but he fulfilled a useful, happy life. I must try to make a fresh beginning next year.”

After Warne's death, Potter moved to the Lake District in northern England. In 1905 she bought a small farm there, and for the next eight years she busied herself writing more books, some set in or around the area. She remained there for the rest of her life, but failing eyesight eventually forced her to stop writing children's books. Instead, she devoted herself to breeding sheep and helping to conserve farms in the district. Thanks to the proceeds from her successful books and a later inheritance, Potter was able to buy many working farms. On her death she left more than 4,000 acres to the National Trust, an organisation that protects historic buildings and countryside in England. As a result, her beloved landscape is now accessible to countless visitors.
from the passage for each answer.

She received lessons at home from a
She disliked the work of some
She practised drawing things she saw when she visited
to have a career in natural history was not realised.

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：童年 - 她在家由_\___\__授课
答案：governess（家庭教师）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “She was educated at home by a governess”，明确提到波特由家庭教师在家授课，因此答案为 “governess”。
题目翻译：童年 - 她用密码写_\___\__
答案：diary（日记）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “when she was 15, she began to keep a diary written in a secret code of her own invention”，明确提到她用密码写日记，因此答案为 “diary”。
题目翻译：童年 - 她不喜欢当时一些_\___\__的作品
答案：artists（艺术家）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “in her diary she expressed herself freely and was critical of the work of several contemporary artists”，“contemporary” 对应 “of the time”，明确提到她批评当时几位艺术家的作品，因此答案为 “artists”。
题目翻译：童年 - 她参观_\___\__时，会临摹所见事物以练习绘画
答案：museums（博物馆）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “gradually improving her work by sketching in museums”，明确提到她通过在博物馆写生提升画技，因此答案为 “museums”。
题目翻译：青年时期 - 她从事自然史工作的_\___\__未能实现
答案：ambition（志向）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “at one time she had an ambition to develop this scientific interest. An uncle tried to help her enrol at the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew... but she was rejected because of her gender”，明确提到她 “立志” 从事自然史相关工作却遭拒绝，因此答案为 “ambition”。
题目翻译：青年时期 - 她的插画被认可对_\___\__研究有帮助
答案：mycology（真菌学）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “she won respect from the scientific establishment for her illustrations and her contribution to mycology, the study of fungi”，明确提到她的插画对真菌学研究有贡献，因此答案为 “mycology”。

items.text:
（1）题目 1：Childhood - She received lessons at home from a \___\___
题目翻译：童年 - 她在家由_\___\__授课
答案：governess（家庭教师）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “She was educated at home by a governess”，明确提到波特由家庭教师在家授课，因此答案为 “governess”。

（2）题目 2：Childhood - She wrote in her \___\___ in code.
题目翻译：童年 - 她用密码写_\___\__
答案：diary（日记）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “when she was 15, she began to keep a diary written in a secret code of her own invention”，明确提到她用密码写日记，因此答案为 “diary”。

（3）题目 3：Childhood - She disliked the work of some \___\___ of the time.
题目翻译：童年 - 她不喜欢当时一些_\___\__的作品
答案：artists（艺术家）
解析：定位 Paragraph 2 中 “in her diary she expressed herself freely and was critical of the work of several contemporary artists”，“contemporary” 对应 “of the time”，明确提到她批评当时几位艺术家的作品，因此答案为 “artists”。

（4）题目 4：Childhood - She practised drawing things she saw when she visited \___\___
题目翻译：童年 - 她参观_\___\__时，会临摹所见事物以练习绘画
答案：museums（博物馆）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “gradually improving her work by sketching in museums”，明确提到她通过在博物馆写生提升画技，因此答案为 “museums”。

（5）题目 5：Life as a young adult - Her \___\___ to have a career in natural history was not realised.
题目翻译：青年时期 - 她从事自然史工作的_\___\__未能实现
答案：ambition（志向）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “at one time she had an ambition to develop this scientific interest. An uncle tried to help her enrol at the Royal Botanic Gardens at Kew... but she was rejected because of her gender”，明确提到她 “立志” 从事自然史相关工作却遭拒绝，因此答案为 “ambition”。

（6）题目 6：Life as a young adult - Her illustrations were recognised as assisting research into \___\___
题目翻译：青年时期 - 她的插画被认可对_\___\__研究有帮助
答案：mycology（真菌学）
解析：定位 Paragraph 3 中 “she won respect from the scientific establishment for her illustrations and her contribution to mycology, the study of fungi”，明确提到她的插画对真菌学研究有贡献，因此答案为 “mycology”。

children.text: Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?
In boxes 7–13 on your answer sheet, write:
if the statement agrees with the information
if the statement contradicts the information
if there is no information about this
The life of a typical married woman at that time appealed to Potter.
Potter's publishers insisted on changing the title of her first book.
The publishers doubted whether Potter's first book would be successful.
Norman Warne suggested Potter include black-and-white illustrations in her first book.
At first, Potter's parents disapproved of Norman Warne as a potential husband.
Potter continued writing children's books until her death.
Potter's gift to the National Trust was the largest in recent times.

explanationSection.text:
题目翻译：当时普通已婚女性的生活对波特有吸引力。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “Fiercely independent, she disliked the idea of being tied to a domestic life that, at that time, consisted mostly of staying at home and raising children”，明确提到波特 “厌恶” 当时已婚女性的居家生活，与题干 “有吸引力” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：波特的出版商坚持更改她首部作品的标题。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：文章仅提到出版商对《彼得兔的故事》的 “销量预期、插画颜色、书籍尺寸” 有讨论，未提及 “更改标题”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
题目翻译：出版商怀疑波特首部作品能否成功。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “the publishers did not expect it to sell many copies. The project was given to the youngest brother in the firm, Norman Warne, as his first assignment-essentially a test”，“did not expect it to sell many copies” 对应 “doubted whether... would be successful”，明确提到出版商不看好销量，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：诺曼・沃恩建议波特在首部作品中加入黑白插画。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “It was Norman who insisted that each illustration should be in colour”，明确提到诺曼坚持 “彩色插画”，与题干 “黑白插画” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：起初，波特的父母不认可诺曼・沃恩作为潜在女婿。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “Potter’s parents disapproved because of his occupation”，明确提到父母因诺曼的职业 “反对” 两人婚事，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。
题目翻译：波特一直创作儿童读物直至去世。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “failing eyesight eventually forced her to stop writing children’s books. Instead, she devoted herself to breeding sheep and helping to conserve farms in the district”，明确提到波特因视力衰退 “停止创作儿童读物”，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。
题目翻译：波特捐赠给国民信托的财产是近年最大规模的捐赠。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 7 仅提到波特捐赠 “4000 多英亩土地”，未与 “近年其他捐赠” 比较 “规模大小”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

items.text:
（1）题目 7：The life of a typical married woman at that time appealed to Potter.
题目翻译：当时普通已婚女性的生活对波特有吸引力。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 4 中 “Fiercely independent, she disliked the idea of being tied to a domestic life that, at that time, consisted mostly of staying at home and raising children”，明确提到波特 “厌恶” 当时已婚女性的居家生活，与题干 “有吸引力” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（2）题目 8：Potter’s publishers insisted on changing the title of her first book.
题目翻译：波特的出版商坚持更改她首部作品的标题。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：文章仅提到出版商对《彼得兔的故事》的 “销量预期、插画颜色、书籍尺寸” 有讨论，未提及 “更改标题”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。

（3）题目 9：The publishers doubted whether Potter’s first book would be successful.
题目翻译：出版商怀疑波特首部作品能否成功。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “the publishers did not expect it to sell many copies. The project was given to the youngest brother in the firm, Norman Warne, as his first assignment-essentially a test”，“did not expect it to sell many copies” 对应 “doubted whether... would be successful”，明确提到出版商不看好销量，因此判定为 TRUE。

（4）题目 10：Norman Warne suggested Potter include black-and-white illustrations in her first book.
题目翻译：诺曼・沃恩建议波特在首部作品中加入黑白插画。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 5 中 “It was Norman who insisted that each illustration should be in colour”，明确提到诺曼坚持 “彩色插画”，与题干 “黑白插画” 描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（5）题目 11：At first, Potter’s parents disapproved of Norman Warne as a potential husband.
题目翻译：起初，波特的父母不认可诺曼・沃恩作为潜在女婿。
答案：TRUE（正确）
解析：定位 Paragraph 6 中 “Potter’s parents disapproved because of his occupation”，明确提到父母因诺曼的职业 “反对” 两人婚事，与题干描述一致，因此判定为 TRUE。

（6）题目 12：Potter continued writing children’s books until her death.
题目翻译：波特一直创作儿童读物直至去世。
答案：FALSE（错误）
解析：定位 Paragraph 7 中 “failing eyesight eventually forced her to stop writing children’s books. Instead, she devoted herself to breeding sheep and helping to conserve farms in the district”，明确提到波特因视力衰退 “停止创作儿童读物”，与题干描述矛盾，因此判定为 FALSE。

（7）题目 13：Potter’s gift to the National Trust was the largest in recent times.
题目翻译：波特捐赠给国民信托的财产是近年最大规模的捐赠。
答案：NOT GIVEN（未提及）
解析：Paragraph 7 仅提到波特捐赠 “4000 多英亩土地”，未与 “近年其他捐赠” 比较 “规模大小”，无相关信息，因此判定为 NOT GIVEN。
